4 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Imagine finding a photo that you posted of yourself from your freshman orientation, reimagined in front of the stage at the Great American State Fair. You didn’t pose for it. You didn’t consent to it. But now you have to explain to your new dorm mates that you don’t know what the fair was like because you were at home in Cincinnati. That is the scenario Meta’s new AI image generator makes possible for anyone with a public Instagram account.
Introducing Muse Image
Muse Image, which Meta rolled out Tuesday, lets users create original images, edit existing photos, and generate ads inside Instagram and Meta’s AI chatbot. However, one policy change buried inside the fine print is now catching hands. That is Meta’s decision to allow the new tool to pull photos from any public Instagram account, without asking first, and turn them into raw material for someone else’s AI-generated image. That means, if your account is public, anyone can reuse your photos as part of an AI-generated creation.
Meta says in a Help Center page that only private accounts are excluded automatically. Everyone else is opted in by default, with minors having their pool of eligible creators restricted to people they follow — and only if their settings allow reuse. Furthermore, Instagram says it will not notify you when someone reuses your content.
This whole issue of cramming AI down users’ throats is becoming a pattern with Meta. They just took it on the chin less than two weeks ago for opting advertisers into AI-generated ads, which didn’t work out great for REI.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

That bike would be an adventure alright 😅The company enables this feature by default and leaves the burden of finding and disabling these settings to users. Not for nothing, it was a similar design choice that drew scrutiny from the Federal Trade Commission when they investigated the company in 2019 and found the platform had misled users about how much control they actually had over their personal information.
How to Opt Out
Meta buried the control several menus deep, but I grabbed some screenshots from our New Yorkie’s Instagram account using my iPhone to walk you through it.
Step 1: Open your Settings
On your profile screen, tap the three horizontal lines in the upper-right corner.
Step 2: Navigate to Sharing and reuse
Searching is faster than browsing.
Step 3: Opt out for posts and/or reels
It’s the Allow people to reuse your content on Instagram and with AI features at Meta options that you want to deactivate.
Caveats
A few caveats are worth knowing before you assume you are covered:

• If someone already used your photos to generate an image before you turned the setting off, that image will not be deleted. Opting out only stops future use.

• The under-18 exclusion depends on Instagram correctly identifying an account as belonging to a teen. If you manage an account for a minor or the teen’s birthdate was entered inaccurately when it was set up, you may want to check out Meta’s Help Center page on Content Setting for Teen Accounts page to batten down the hatches.
Why This Keeps Happening
Public wariness about AI is already high. A recent Pew Research Center survey found 40% of respondents expect AI to have a negative impact on society, and 31% said they anticipate a negative personal impact as well. So the idea of corralling users to opt in to more AI slop on social media probably wasn’t met with enthusiasm, even if Meta were batting 1000. But Meta CEO Mark Zuckerberg himself admitted that Meta’s innovation momentum has been lagging, with Meta Chief Technology Officer Andrew “Boz” Bosworth reportedly acknowledging to staff that employee morale was very low following a spate of AI-related PR nightmares.
Meta needs a win. The company hasn’t kept pace with the dizzying pace of other AI labs, such as Anthropic, OpenAI, and Google, and I suspect they may feel the need to bolster their AI engagement metrics for investors. Regardless of their rationale, the inmates have taken over the asylum, and the best we can do is trudge through fine print to figure out how to protect ourselves as much as possible from the ongoing intrusion of unwanted AI.
